questions.question: Define the American Dream in your own words (2-3 sentences).
questions.sampleAnswer: The American Dream is the belief that everyone — regardless of where they were born or what social class they come from — has a fair chance to achieve a better life through hard work. Its core values include freedom, equality of opportunity, and the right to seek one's own happiness.

questions.question: Name two pro-arguments and two contra-arguments about whether the American Dream still works today.
questions.sampleAnswer: PRO: (1) The USA still attracts about a million legal migrants per year, suggesting the Dream is still seen as real. (2) Tech leaders like Sundar Pichai (Google) show migrants can reach the very top. CONTRA: (1) Studies (e.g. Raj Chetty) show US social mobility is lower than in Germany or Canada — so being born poor usually means staying poor. (2) Wealth inequality is huge: the top 1% own more than the bottom 50% combined.

questions.question: Why is it a mistake to define the American Dream only as 'becoming rich'?
questions.sampleAnswer: Reducing the Dream to wealth misses its core. The original idea included freedom, equality of opportunity, and the right to pursue happiness — not just money. Self-realisation, religious freedom, and dignity for everyone are equally important parts. Treating the Dream as money-only makes it both narrower and easier to dismiss.

questions.question: Which event is most associated with the murder that helped trigger the modern BLM movement?
What were the Jim Crow Laws and where did they apply?
questions.sampleAnswer: The Jim Crow Laws were a set of state and local laws in the Southern USA that enforced racial segregation. They required separate schools, hospitals, public facilities, drinking fountains, and bus seats for Black and white people. They lasted from after the Civil War until the 1960s, when the Civil Rights Act outlawed them.

questions.question: Compare the strategies of Martin Luther King Jr. and Malcolm X.
questions.sampleAnswer: MLK followed nonviolent resistance inspired by Gandhi — sit-ins, marches, boycotts. He aimed for integration into the American system. Malcolm X, originally part of the Nation of Islam, allowed self-defence ('by any means necessary') and was sceptical of integration, preferring Black self-reliance and pride. Their methods were different but together created a balance: King's moderation looked acceptable next to Malcolm X's radicalism, increasing the pressure on white politicians.

questions.question: What did the Civil Rights Act of 1964 achieve?
questions.sampleAnswer: It outlawed discrimination based on race, colour, religion, sex, or national origin in public facilities, schools and employment. It ended legal segregation in restaurants, hotels and public places. It was a milestone — the formal end of the Jim Crow era — even if real-world equality was not achieved automatically.

questions.question: Why is BLM seen as a continuation of the Civil Rights Movement?
questions.sampleAnswer: BLM continues the Civil Rights Movement's central goal: equal treatment for Black Americans. The struggles are similar — racial profiling, police violence, unequal opportunity — but the methods evolved. Where MLK marched and gave speeches, BLM uses smartphones, video evidence (e.g. of George Floyd's death) and social media hashtags to mobilise quickly and globally. The grievance is the same, the megaphone is digital.

questions.question: Why was the Voting Rights Act (1965) often called even more important than the Civil Rights Act (1964)?
questions.sampleAnswer: The Civil Rights Act outlawed segregation, but it didn't automatically give Black citizens the political power to defend their rights. The Voting Rights Act removed the literacy tests and poll taxes used to keep Black people from voting in the South. With actual access to the ballot box, Black communities could hold politicians accountable, elect their own representatives, and secure further reforms. Without voting power, legal rights are easier to ignore — that's why many historians see the 1965 Act as the more transformative one.

questions.question: Name the three branches of US government and what each does.
questions.sampleAnswer: 1) Legislative — Congress (Senate + House of Representatives): makes laws. 2) Executive — President + cabinet: enforces laws and runs the country. 3) Judicial — Supreme Court + lower federal courts: interprets laws and checks them against the Constitution.

questions.question: Explain 'checks and balances' with one concrete example.
questions.sampleAnswer: Checks and balances means that each branch can limit the power of the others. Example: Congress passes a law. The President can veto it. Congress can override the veto with a two-thirds majority in both chambers. Even if the law passes, the Supreme Court can declare it unconstitutional. So no branch acts alone — each is checked by the others.

questions.question: Why can a candidate win the US presidency with fewer popular votes than their opponent?
questions.sampleAnswer: Because the President is not chosen by the national popular vote, but by 538 electors in the Electoral College. Each state has a fixed number of electors and (in almost every state) all of them go to whoever wins that state — even narrowly. A candidate who wins many small states by tiny margins can collect 270+ electoral votes while losing the overall national popular vote, as in 2000 (Bush) and 2016 (Trump).

questions.question: Why are Supreme Court appointments so politically important in the USA?
questions.sampleAnswer: Supreme Court justices serve for life and have the power of judicial review — they can strike down laws and decisions as unconstitutional. A single appointment can shape the Court's ideological direction for thirty or more years, influencing decisions on abortion, gun rights, voting rules, and more. That's why every president tries to appoint justices who share their views, and why every Senate confirmation hearing is a major political battle.

options: I think the speech is good.
Through anaphora, antithesis, and personal imagery, MLK builds emotional momentum that turns an abstract civil-rights demand into a shared moral vision.

options: without a quote the claim is unsupported — it's just a statement, not analysis
to look like a research paper

questions.question: List the four steps of analysing a political speech.
questions.sampleAnswer: 1) Context — Who spoke when, where, and why? 2) Content — What is the main message? 3) Structure & Style — How is the speech built and which rhetorical devices are used? 4) Effect — What impact does the speaker want on the audience?

questions.question: What is anaphora? Give one famous example.
questions.sampleAnswer: Anaphora is the repetition of the same word or phrase at the beginning of consecutive clauses or sentences. The most famous example is MLK's repeated 'I have a dream that…' in his 1963 speech, which builds rhythm and emotional momentum.

questions.question: Analyse Kennedy's line: 'Ask not what your country can do for you — ask what you can do for your country.' Identify two devices and explain their effect.
questions.sampleAnswer: The line contains antithesis (what your country can do for you ↔ what you can do for your country) and parallelism (two clauses with the same structure). The antithesis flips the expectation and makes the contrast memorable; the parallelism creates rhythm. The imperative 'Ask' addresses the audience directly and turns them into active citizens. Together, the devices push the central message — Cold War USA needs active service rather than passive expectation — deep into the listener's memory.

questions.question: Why is it a mistake to retell the content of a speech instead of analysing it?
questions.sampleAnswer: An exam question on speech analysis isn't asking what was said — it's asking how it was said and why it works. Just retelling the content shows reading comprehension, not analytic skill. To answer fully, you need to identify rhetorical devices, support each with a direct quote, and explain the intended effect on the audience.

questions.question: Pathos, ethos, and logos — give one example of each from a typical political speech.
questions.sampleAnswer: Pathos (emotion): MLK's image of 'little black boys and black girls' triggers empathy with parents. Ethos (credibility): 'As your President, I have seen…' builds the speaker's authority. Logos (logic): 'Last year, GDP grew by 4% — these policies are working' uses data to convince. Effective speeches usually mix all three.

questions.question: Imagine you are analysing the line: 'My fellow Americans, this is our moment, this is our time.' Identify the devices and explain their effects.
questions.sampleAnswer: Devices: (1) Direct address ('My fellow Americans') instantly creates a personal connection. (2) Inclusive 'our' includes speaker and audience as one collective — no 'me vs. you'. (3) Anaphora ('this is our moment, this is our time') repeats the structure for rhythm and emphasis. (4) Parallelism between the two clauses tightens the rhetoric. Effect: the audience feels pulled into a shared mission, owning the moment with the speaker rather than being told about it. Such phrasing is typical of Obama-style campaign rhetoric — building unity through grammatical inclusion before any actual argument is made.
